doc_title: Biosafety in Microbiological and Biomedical Laboratories—6th Edition

steps.text: Identify hazardous characteristics of the agent and perform an assessment of the inherent risk.
Make a determination of the appropriate Biosafety Level and select additional precautions indicated by the risk assessment.
Review the risk assessment and selected safeguards with a biosafety professional, subject matter expert, and the IBC or equivalent resource before implementation of the controls.
Conduct laboratory work using the selected Biosafety Level and precautions.
Evaluate the proficiencies of staff regarding safe practices and the integrity of safety equipment.
Revisit regularly and verify risk management strategies and determine if changes are necessary.

preconditions.text: Before research involving biological hazards begins, stakeholders must have plans in place for providing occupational health support for staff.

Prior to beginning an animal study, animal protocols must be reviewed and approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) and the Institutional Biosafety Committee (IBC), as appropriate.

constraints.text: Gloves must be worn to protect hands from exposure to hazardous materials.
Mouth pipetting is prohibited; mechanical pipetting devices must be used.
Do not wash or reuse disposable gloves, and dispose of used gloves with other contaminated laboratory waste.
Long hair must be restrained so that it cannot contact hands, specimens, containers, or equipment.
Eating, drinking, smoking, handling contact lenses, applying cosmetics, and storing food for human consumption are not permitted in laboratory areas.
In case of a potential hazard exposure, the staff member must immediately perform proper first aid and follow all established agent-specific protocols.
